text: This is a video presentation of laparoscopic supra-elevator posterior accentuation in a case of previous laparoscopic ovarian transposition.

Our patient is a twenty eight year old lady with locally advanced rectal cancer with biopsy suggestive of moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with MRI suggestive of involvement of the anterior peritoneal reflection.
The patient is planned for neoadjuvant chemoradiation prior to which she underwent laparoscopic ovarian transposition.
The MRI done post neoadjuvant chemo radiation shows persistent involvement of the anterior peritoneal reflection and the patient is planned for laparoscopic supra levator posterior accentration.
The video displays the standard port positions used for laparoscopic rectal surgeries, showing the arrangement of trocars on the abdominal surface.
The surgeon initiates dissection by creating a plane below the inferior mesenteric vein, proceeding medially to laterally along the mesocolic fascia.
The surgeon clips and divides the inferior mesenteric vein near the DJ flexure, using surgical instruments to isolate and cut the vessel.
Adhesions of the sigmoid colon are visible, likely resulting from the previous laparoscopic ovarian transposition procedure.
The surgeon performs adhesiolysis using a harmonic scalpel to carefully separate adhesions in the surgical field.
The surgeon retracts the fully mobilized sigmoid colon ventrally and incises the medial peritoneal fold, exposing underlying structures.
The surgeon continues dissection in the avascular plane below the inferior mesenteric artery, moving from medial to lateral and extending the dissection inferiorly into the pelvis posteriorly.
The surgeon bares the inferior mesenteric artery at its root, then performs high ligation, clipping, and division of the artery.
The surgeon continues dissection from medial to lateral along the mesocolic fascia, progressing towards the lateral peritoneal attachment.
The surgeon makes a lateral peritoneal cut, mobilizing the descending colon and splenic flexure, with visible separation of these structures from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon begins the pelvic dissection by scoring the pelvic peritoneum along the iliac vessels, marking the initial incision lines in the pelvic region.
The surgeon reaches the round ligament, which is dissected, clipped, and divided, continuing the dissection process.
The surgeon continues the pelvic dissection, creating the para-rectal and para-bicycle spaces by carefully separating tissue planes in the pelvic region.
The surgeon begins dissecting the left ureter, carefully following its course until reaching the ureterovesical junction.
The surgeon continues the ureteric dissection towards the UV junction and encounters the uterine artery, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon clips and divides the uterine artery, which visually separates the para-rectal and para-vesical spaces within the pelvic region.
Following the division of the uterine artery, the surgeon continues dissecting the ureter, progressing towards the uretero-vesical junction.
The surgeon incises the uterovasical fold of peritoneum, separating the bladder from the lower uterine segment and cervix, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the dissection.
The surgeon repeats the dissection procedure on the right side, mirroring the steps previously performed on the left ureter towards the uretero-vasical junction.
The surgeon begins dissecting the ureter near the pelvic inlet, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon dissects the round ligament, applying clips before dividing it.
The surgeon continues the ureteric dissection further into the pelvis, progressing until the uterine artery is identified.
The surgeon clips and divides the opposite uterine artery, completing the dissection on the contralateral side within the pelvic region.
The surgeon continues the posterior dissection, advancing towards the pelvic floor, carefully separating tissue layers in the pelvic region.
The surgeon uses a monopolar cautery to cut the antevaginal wall, visibly opening the vaginal vault.
The surgeon initiates the lateral dissection of the rectum, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues within the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon identifies the hypogastric nerve, marked by an arrow, and ensures its preservation during the dissection in the pelvic region.
After completing the lateral dissection to the pelvic floor, the surgeon incises the posterior vaginal wall to finalize the vaginal cut.
Following the incision of the posterior vaginal wall, the surgeon continues dissection in the rectovaginal septum to fully mobilize the rectum.
The surgeon completes the total mesorectal excision by fully mobilizing the rectum down to the pelvic floor, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon completes the rectal mobilization and uses an endostapler to divide the distal end of the rectal specimen.
The surgeon sutures the vaginal vault using a V-lock suture, securing the tissue with visible precision.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis using a circular stapler, joining the rectal tissue ends together.
The surgeon confirms the alignment of the colon and fires the stapler to complete the anastomosis.
The surgeon inserts Jackson-Pratt drains into the pelvis and performs a covering ileostomy, visually securing the drains and creating the ileostomy site.
Patient had an uneventful post-operative recovery and discharged on post-operative day six.

The final list of pathology revealed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with negative proximal and distal resection margins with negative circumferential resection margin and three out of fifteen lymph nodes positive
